editorial.h2: A low-stakes playground for who you could be
editorial.lead: Try on versions of yourself without booking a studio, buying a wardrobe, or committing to a new haircut. Credits buy attempts, not guarantees.

bullets: Sketch alternate selves before spending money on clothes or shoots.
Generate profile-ready frames alongside playful, story-driven shots.
Iterate quickly: same you, different pose, background, or outfit direction.
Treat credits as sketch paper—some pages are keepers, some are not.
Built for personal portraits, not for SKU-perfect product photography.

buildLook.h2: Build your look from seed photos
buildLook.lead: Start with a small set of seed images—usually a full-body frame plus a clear face reference. PublicSelf turns them into a reusable visual identity.
buildLook.imageAlt: Building a personal look from seed photos for an AI portrait session

bullets: Skin tone, facial structure, hairline, and body proportions.
A consistent target so later generations stay recognizable.
Better lighting and clearer angles mean fewer surprises.
Stand in reasonable, even light—no extreme filters.
Show your face unobstructed; avoid hats, masks, heavy makeup.
A phone camera is fine; intention matters more than gear.
Save a look when one direction feels right.
Reuse it across sessions instead of re-uploading every time.
Reusable looks make later shoots fast: you direct, not rebuild.

photoshoot.h2: Your AI photoshoot: direction, not dice rolls
photoshoot.lead: Behave like a creative director. Choose pose, expression, background, and optional items from the in-app library. The output is a fresh frame of you.
photoshoot.imageAlt: Directing an AI photoshoot with pose, expression, background, and optional items

bullets: Pose and framing—headshot, three-quarter, or playful editorial.
Expression and gaze—approachable, focused, or candid energy.
Background and scene—studio, street, interior, or cinematic.
Optional items from the curated library: bags, watches, outerwear cues.
Restrained, well-lit frames for LinkedIn and CV.
Louder, more expressive shots for Instagram or portfolios.
Quiet, private experiments you may never post.

blocks.h3: When a shot is close but not quite
bullets: Tweak pose or simplify the background before re-rolling.
Reduce clutter—hands, jewelry, and thin straps trip up every generator.
Small adjustments beat brute-forcing identical settings.

experiment.h2: Experiment, iterate, and Tune when it's on
experiment.lead: Treat generations as drafts: review, laugh, adjust. Use Tune (when enabled for your workspace) to nudge a nearly-right frame closer.
experiment.imageAlt: Iterating on AI portrait results and optional Tune edits with garment references

bullets: Credits are tries, not contracts—batches beat one mega-prompt.
Small moves surface happy accidents you didn't plan for.
If you need every frame perfect on the first try, no AI tool will feel comfortable.
Targeted edits on an existing result—often using a reference garment photo.
Swap or refine a clothing layer without rebuilding the whole scene.
May be disabled by configuration; not guaranteed on every account.
Not available alongside trained / LoRA identity modes.
AI can drift; accessories may glue oddly; text on clothing can gibberish.
Hosting is ephemeral—download anything you want to keep.
If you share AI-generated images, label them where your platform expects it.

faq.intro: Straight answers about how PublicSelf behaves in practice—so you know what to expect before you spend time or credits.

items.q: What is PublicSelf in one sentence?
items.a: PublicSelf is a web app that builds a look from your seed photos, then runs an AI-directed virtual photoshoot where you control pose, expression, background, and optional items—plus targeted Tune edits when that mode is enabled for your workspace.

items.q: Do I need professional photos to start?
items.a: No. Clear phone photos with decent lighting work. You will get better consistency if your face is visible, angles are reasonable, and you avoid heavy beauty filters that erase the traits you want preserved.

items.q: How well does PublicSelf preserve my identity?
items.a: The system tries to keep you recognizable across variations, but no model is perfect. Glasses, hats, heavy makeup, or low-resolution uploads can confuse the pipeline. If something drifts, adjust your seeds, simplify accessories, or regenerate—that is normal, not a sign you are using it wrong.

items.q: Can I pull any random product image from the internet as a prop?
items.a: No—do not expect a free-form 'paste any URL' pipeline. Reliable results come from the in-app item library and, where Tune is available, from reference images you supply through the supported upload flow, not from arbitrary scraping.

Tune is an edit pass on an existing result. When enabled, it can combine text instructions with a reference photo of a garment (for example a jacket or shoes) so you can nudge clothing without rebuilding the entire shot. Availability depends on site configuration, and it is not compatible with trained / LoRA identity mode in the app.

No. AI portraits sometimes miss hands, accessories, or fine fabric detail. Expect to iterate. Small setting changes—a different pose, a simpler background, less clutter—usually beat spamming identical regenerations.

items.q: Why is there a separate 'Build your look' step?
items.a: Skipping the look step might work for abstract art, but PublicSelf is optimized for photographic plausibility. A grounded look file keeps later poses believable: hands, shoulders, and fabric behave more coherently when the base identity is stable. Patience in the builder pays off in the photoshoot.

items.q: Is this meant for e-commerce product photography?
items.a: No. PublicSelf targets personal identity exploration and portrait-style outputs. If you need SKU-accurate merchandise shots, use tooling built for catalogs.

items.q: Can I use the images on LinkedIn or Instagram?
items.a: Many users do, but you are responsible for following each platform's rules—including disclosure expectations for synthetic or AI-assisted media where they apply.

items.q: Do you keep my files forever?
items.a: Do not rely on us as long-term storage. Download what you care about; the product is explicit that hosting is ephemeral by default.
Credits pay for generation attempts. Bundles are sold on the landing page; occasional signup promotions may add starter credits. Exact numbers depend on live configuration.

items.q: Is PublicSelf trying to replace photographers?
items.a: No. The goal is a private studio on demand for individual experimentation: try ten ideas in an evening, download what you like, move on. Real photographers still matter for the moments that warrant them.
